Summary: In chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), NOTCH1 mutations promote CLL cell proliferation. Trisomy 12 CLL is associated with unmutated IGHV and with NOTCH1 mutations. NOTCH1 mutations are associated with inferior outcomes with chemoimmunotherapy, with single-agent venetoclax, and with single-agent ibrutinib. NOTCH1 mutations are associated with a higher risk of transformation. In the VenR arm, NOTCH1 mutations were associated with lower uMRD rates at end of treatment but did not show an adverse impact on progression-free survival in the current analysis. Further studies of larger cohorts are needed to determine the independent contribution of these biologic factors.

Summary:
Systematic elaboration detected.

NOTCH1 is a receptor in mammals. NOTCH signaling regulates differentiation, proliferation, and apoptosis. In T-ALL, activating NOTCH1 mutations occur in over 50–60% of cases. 8–30% of T-ALLs harbor mutations in FBXW7, which lead to increased NOTCH1 protein stability; paracrine mechanisms that result in NOTCH1 or NOTCH3 signaling upregulation, rare mutations in NOTCH3, and aberrant expression of the NOTCH ligand DLL4 may also contribute, so the majority of T-ALL cases have hyper-activated NOTCH signaling. NOTCH1 acts as an oncogene in T-ALL and regulates NF-κB and MYC. NOTCH1 regulates Hes and Hey/Hers transcriptional repressors; Hes1 suppresses PTEN and CYLD and sustains PI3K-AKT signaling and NF-κB activation. Hes1 represses BBC3/Puma and negatively regulates apoptosis of T-ALL cells. NOTCH1 regulates CCR5, CCR7, and CCR9 to influence migration. In B-CLL, activating NOTCH1 mutations occur in ≈8% at diagnosis, ≈30% in Richter transformation, and ≈20% in chemo-refractory cases and cluster in the PEST domain; a 2-bp frameshift deletion (ΔCT7544–7545, P2515fs) represents ≈80% of mutant cases and is a potential screening and therapy target. NOTCH1‑mutant B-CLL at diagnosis has poor prognosis similar to TP53‑disrupted cases. The functional role of NOTCH1 in B-CLL is unclear; ICN1 is expressed in ≈50% of B-CLL cases with wild‑type NOTCH1, indicating alternative activation. Independent of mutation status, ICN1-positive B-CLL cases express a NOTCH1 transcriptional signature and are sensitive to γ-secretase inhibitors. ICN1-positive cases regulate CCND3, BCL2, MCL1, BCR signaling pathway genes, and NF-κB pathway genes.
Reference: Piovan, E., Tosello, V., Amadori, A., & Zanovello, P. (2018). Chemotactic Cues for NOTCH1-Dependent Leukemia. Front Immunol, 9, 633. https://doi.org/10.3389/fimmu.2018.00633

Summary: Systematic elaboration of NOTCH1 role. NOTCH1 normally reads thymic microenvironment signals that commit lymphoid progenitors to T-cell fate. In T-ALL, activating NOTCH1 mutations occur in over 65% of cases. NOTCH1 receptor mutations induce constitutive NOTCH1 activation or impair degradation of active NOTCH1. NOTCH1 is a type I transmembrane protein that requires intramembrane γ-secretase cleavage for activation. Constitutively active NOTCH1 activates broad cell growth and metabolism programs. Constitutively active NOTCH1 activates MYC and facilitates increased PI3K signaling. Inhibition of NOTCH1 induces a starvation response that suppresses anabolic pathways and upregulates cellular catabolism and autophagy in leukemia. NOTCH1 inhibition downregulates glycolysis and increases leukemia cell dependence on glutaminolysis. Genetic inactivation of glutaminase enhances the therapeutic effects of γ-secretase inhibitors. Pharmacologic inhibition of glutaminolysis with BPTES is highly synergistic with NOTCH1 inhibition in preclinical animal models.
Reference: Ferrando, A. (2018). Can one target T-cell ALL?. Best Pract Res Clin Haematol, 31(4), 361-366. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.beha.2018.10.001

Summary: Systematic elaboration. In T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (T-ALL), more than 50% of cases have DNA alterations that cause enhanced NOTCH1 pathway activation. Constitutive expression of the NOTCH1-ΔE variant, which lacks the extracellular domain, leads to overactive NOTCH1 signaling and enforces T-cell differentiation. Notch dimerization–dependent signaling drives a gene signature that includes HES4, and HES4 enforces Δ133p53 expression, blocks proapoptotic p53 target genes, and induces BCL2L1 (Bcl-xL). NOTCH1 dimeric signaling is crucial for leukemogenesis of human cord blood hematopoietic stem/progenitor cells and for maintenance of Notch dimerization–dependent patient-derived T-ALL xenografts, whereas a dimerization-defective NOTCH1-ΔE-R1984A failed to generate T-ALL–like leukemias in NSG mice. Cell subsets with Notch dimerization–dependent gene signatures, which include HES4, directly associated with cell markers of poor clinical outcome in primary T-ALL samples.
Reference: Tamiro, F., Padovano, C., De Santis, E., Di Iasio, S., Sansico, D. F., Canistro, V., Colucci, M., Di Nunzio, C., Bruno, G., Doshi, K., Totaro, A., Gu, E., Santodirocco, M., Weng, A.
